text: Last class you said: To sum up, I am happy to take any questions.
Now we ask: How do you answer a hard question without losing your confidence?

Could you tell me what the budget is?
May I ask why you think that?
Do you know if the report is ready?
I was wondering if you could clarify that.
Thank you for your talk, Doctor. May I ask why the training results improved so much this year?
Thank you for bringing that up. That is a fair point. The main reason is our new online training program.
I have a follow-up about the budget and the timeline. Could you tell me how both will change next year?
Your question has two parts, so let me break it down. First, I will address the budget, and then the timeline.
One last question: what was the exact figure two years ago?
That is a fair point, but I do not have that figure off hand. Let me get back to you by email tomorrow.
Sorry, could you rephrase that?
Could you tell me what you mean?
That is a fair point. Let me address it.
I do not have that off hand. Let me get back to you by email.

Hello Doctor Nilo, this is Sofia again from the FIFA Leadership in Football program office. Last week you gave your presentation, and it went very well. Today I want to prepare you for the hardest part: the question and answer session at the end. When you finish your talk and invite questions, the committee will challenge you, so here is some friendly advice. First, always listen to the whole question before you answer; never interrupt the person. Second, if a question is not clear, ask the speaker to rephrase it. You can say, sorry, could you rephrase that, or, in other words, are you asking about the budget? This buys you a few seconds to think. Third, be polite and indirect. Instead of a direct question like, what do you mean, say, could you tell me what you mean, or, may I ask why you think that. Indirect questions sound softer and more professional. Fourth, if you do not know an answer, do not guess. It is perfectly fine to say, that is a fair point, but I do not have that figure off hand. Let me get back to you by email after the meeting. Fifth, when a question is complex, break it down into smaller parts and address one part at a time. And finally, always thank the person for the question before you answer. Remember, Doctor, a good answer is calm, short, and honest. You do not need to know everything. You only need to handle each question with confidence. Good luck in the hot seat.

Let me tell you how the question and answer session went after my presentation to the compliance committee. When I finished, I said, thank you, I am happy to take any questions, and I will be honest, that moment always makes me a little nervous. The first question was clear, so I thanked the person and answered it in two sentences. The second question was long and had two parts, so I broke it down. I said, let me address your first point about the budget, and then I will come back to the timeline. That helped me stay organized. Then a committee member asked something I did not fully understand, so I did not pretend. I asked him to rephrase the question, and I said, in other words, are you asking about the new audit procedure? He said yes, and then I could answer well. The hardest moment came near the end. Someone asked for an exact figure from two years ago, and I did not have it off hand. Instead of guessing, I said, that is a fair point, but I do not have that number with me right now. Let me get back to you by email tomorrow. The committee respected that honest answer. To handle the questions politely, I used indirect forms all the time. Instead of saying, why do you think that, I said, may I ask why you think that. It sounded much softer. When the session ended, the chair thanked me, and for the first time I felt that I could handle a tough Q and A in English with confidence.

Here is how to handle a question well. First, thank the person for the question. Then, if it is not clear, ask them to rephrase it. After that, if the question is long, break it down into parts. Next, address each part and answer politely. Finally, if you do not have the answer off hand, offer to get back to them later.
Could you tell me what the budget is?
That is a fair point. Let me address it.
I do not have that off hand. Let me get back to you.
Could you tell me what you mean?
Your question has two parts, so let me break it down.
